title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: LCFS Credit Volumes Witness a Spike WoW; All RIN Prices Rise
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights

(9th February â€“ 15th February 2026)
  • A total of 1,086,091 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, an increase of 88.46% week-on-week (WoW), while all non-zero trades increased 120.36% WoW and a total of 1,706,955 non-zero credits were traded in CA LCFS.
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price stood at $71.03/MT for Type 1 transfers, up by 10.60% WoW; prices of all non-zero transfers rose by 6.19%, price stood at $67.39/MT.
  • The average LCFS spot credit price rose by 1.76% WoW; on 17th February, the spot price stood at $71.50/MT, and the benchmark price stood at $74.00/MT.
  • D3 RIN prices rose by 1.26% WoW, while D4, D5 and D6 RIN prices increased by 7.58%, 7.63% and 7.03% WoW respectively.

cCarbonâ€™s Market View

Last week, under the Washington Clean Fuel Standard, regulated entities and eligible participants can now apply for heavy-duty Capacity Credits for Fast Charging Infrastructure (FCI) and Hydrogen Refueling Infrastructure (HRI), aimed at encouraging investment in clean fuel production and expanding heavy-duty zero-emission transportation infrastructure capacity.

CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume Trend

[caption id="attachment_613392" align="alignnone" width="704"] Table 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average Type 1 transfer price increased by 10.60% WoW, rising from $64.22 to $71.03, while all non-zero transfer prices moved up from $63.46 to $67.39, a 6.19% WoW gain. Type 1 trades totaled 1,086,091 credits, reflecting an 88.46% WoW increase and total non-zero transaction volumes rose by 120.36% WoW, reaching 1,706,955 credits for the week. [caption id="attachment_613393" align="alignnone" width="1200"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption]

CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

[caption id="attachment_613394" align="alignnone" width="1200"] Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption] Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices rose by 1.76% WoW, reaching $71.72/MT. On 17th February, the spot price stood at $71.50/MT. The benchmark price, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2026, stood at $74.00/MT on 17th February.

LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

[caption id="attachment_613395" align="alignnone" width="1200"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption] As per the data reported by CARB last week, a total of 87 trades took place in the market, including 23 high-volume transactions. The highest reported transfer price was $73.50 each for three Type 1 transactions involving 5,500, 10,000, and 20,000 credits. The lowest transfer price was $51.50 for a Type 2 transaction covering 5,000 credits. The largest trade by volume was a Type 2 transaction of 135,000 credits priced at $60.00, while the smallest was a Type 2 transfer of 201 credits priced at $64.00.

CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_613396" align="alignnone" width="1200"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average Type 1 spot price stood at $68.67 in February so far, rising from $58.59 in January. As of 14th February 2026, trade volumes for the month reached 2.48M.

CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_613397" align="alignnone" width="1200"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption] The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls. Across 9th February â€“ 15th February 2026, total VPT rose by 7.14% WoW, with a rise in all non-zero credit prices for the last week.

Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN Weekly Price Trend

RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices rose by 1.26% WoW, while D4, D5 and D6 RIN prices increased by 7.58%, 7.63% and 7.03% WoW respectively. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel

Upcoming DatesÂ 

Key Clean Fuels Market Developments This Week

  • ZERO Fleet Rebate Program- Class 3 Vehicle Funding Limit Established: The Oregon Department of Environmental Qualityâ€™s Zero-Emissions Rebates for Oregon Fleets, or ZERO Fleet Rebate, Program established a limit on the amount of funding available to Class 3 vehicle rebates. The funding cap for Class 3 rebates is 12% of the total funding allotment, which comes to a little over $2 million. Once this percentage is fully committed, new Class 3 rebate applications will no longer be accepted.
  • Canada Advances Auto Strategy with $97M for EV Charging and Clean Fuel Projects: Following the launch of its new Automotive Strategy on February 5, 2026, the Government of Canada has announced over $97 million in funding for 155 clean transportation projects. A major portion, $84.4 million, is allocated to installing more than 8,000 electric vehicle (EV) chargers across the country through the Zero Emission Vehicle Infrastructure Program, adding to the 30,000 already installed. The strategy also includes a $1.5 billion investment via the Canada Infrastructure Bank for charging and hydrogen refuelling infrastructure. Additionally, $5.7 million will support the Green Freight Program to help fleets adopt low-carbon alternative fuel vehicles and repower existing ones.
  • Washington: Department of Ecology Opens Heavy-duty Capacity Credit applications: Regulated entities and eligible participants can now apply for heavy-duty Capacity Credits for Fast Charging Infrastructure (FCI) and Hydrogen Refueling Infrastructure (HRI) under Washingtonâ€™s Clean Fuel Standard. These credits are designed to support investments that expand clean fuel production and increase heavy-duty transportation infrastructure capacity. Heavy-duty Capacity Credits are available to Clean Fuel Standard participants and other eligible entities that invest in projects that expand clean fuel production or delivery capacity in Washington. To apply, visit the CFS Program Documents page and download the FCI or HRI application templates.
  • Ministry of Energy and Climate Solutions Revises Co-Processing Methodology under BC LCFS : BC LCFS releases document provides guidance on how to determine the renewable fraction of co-processed fuel and outlines acceptable methods for determining the inputs required to model the renewable fuelâ€™s CI. This document applies only to coprocessing that occurs in a refinery. It also focuses specifically on methodologies and requirements for coprocessed fuels. It does not provide general guidance on CI application procedures or modelling and should be used in conjunction with the guidance provided in RLCF-008: Carbon Intensity Applications and the applicable â€“ pathway specific â€“ GHGenius user guides available on the Renewable and low carbon fuel resources page.
